question_text: A sealed container holds water at 20°C. The container is then heated to 100°C and the water boils. However, if the same sealed container is heated to 120°C, no more water boils—instead, the pressure inside increases significantly. Which statement best explains why this happens?

options.label: A
options.text: The water is too hot to boil anymore, so it stays as liquid and pressure increases instead.

options.label: B
options.text: The boiling point increases with pressure in a sealed container. At high pressure, water cannot escape as steam, so pressure builds up rather than the water boiling away.

options.label: C
options.text: All the water has already boiled at 100°C, so there is nothing left to boil at 120°C.

options.label: D
options.text: Water molecules move too fast at 120°C to form steam bubbles.

explanation: In a sealed container, as pressure increases, the boiling point of water rises above 100°C. Since the water cannot escape as steam, the pressure builds up instead. This is why pressure cookers work—higher pressure allows water to stay liquid at temperatures above 100°C.
